headline: The Hormuz Chokepoint: How 21 Million Barrels a Day Vanished from Global Markets
subheadline: For the first time in modern history, both major Middle Eastern maritime corridors are simultaneously closed to commercial traffic

body.content: The numbers are staggering in their simplicity: 21 million barrels of oil per day and approximately 150 billion cubic meters of liquefied natural gas per year have been removed from global markets by the closure of a waterway barely 33 kilometers wide at its narrowest point.

body.heading: A Bottleneck the World Built Its Energy System Around
body.content: The Strait of Hormuz has always been the single most consequential chokepoint in global energy infrastructure. Roughly 20 to 30 percent of all seaborne oil passes through its shipping lanes on any given day. For decades, the theoretical risk of its closure has been a staple of energy security analysis — a scenario gamed in think tanks and war colleges but never actually realized. That changed on March 1, when Iran, reeling from the US-Israeli bombardment, deployed its remaining naval assets to block commercial shipping. VLCC tanker rates have surged to all-time highs. Insurance premiums for Gulf-origin cargoes have become prohibitive. The practical effect is a complete shutdown of maritime energy exports from the Persian Gulf.

body.heading: The Dual Closure: Hormuz and Suez
body.content: What makes the current situation historically unprecedented is that it is not just the Strait of Hormuz. Houthi forces in Yemen continue to disrupt Red Sea and Suez Canal traffic, a campaign that has been running for over a year. For the first time in modern history, both of the Middle East's critical maritime corridors are simultaneously closed or severely disrupted. The Cape of Good Hope rerouting that shippers adopted to avoid the Red Sea now applies to Hormuz traffic as well, adding two to three weeks to transit times and compounding freight costs. The six Gulf producers most directly affected — Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Oman, and Iraq — collectively account for roughly a quarter of global oil production. Saudi Arabia has partially activated its PETROLINE East-West pipeline to divert some crude to Red Sea terminals, but the pipeline's capacity of 5 million barrels per day covers only a fraction of the kingdom's normal export volume.

body.heading: Winners and Losers
body.content: The Hormuz closure has created an immediate bifurcation in the global economy between energy exporters with non-Hormuz routes and energy importers dependent on Gulf supplies. Norway, Brazil, Kazakhstan, and Azerbaijan — all significant producers whose exports bypass the strait entirely — are receiving windfall revenues. Australia's LNG exports have become dramatically more valuable as Qatari supply, which represents roughly 20 percent of global LNG trade, goes offline. On the losing side, the damage is concentrated in Asia. South Korea imports 70 percent of its oil through Hormuz-dependent routes. Japan's energy independence stands at just 10 percent. India imports 85 percent of its crude via routes that transit or originate from the Gulf. Even China, which has diversified its supply more aggressively than any other major importer, faces significant disruption.

body.heading: The Price Signal
body.content: Brent crude has surged 36 percent year-to-date to $80 per barrel — a number that understates the spot-market panic, where individual cargoes of non-Hormuz crude are trading at substantial premiums. Gold has hit a record $5,413 per ounce as investors seek hard-asset protection against the inflationary shock. Analyst consensus is diverging sharply on where prices go from here: Goldman Sachs models $110 if disruptions persist through April, while JPMorgan's worst-case scenario reaches $120 to $130 for sustained closure beyond sixty days. The critical variable is duration. Every week of closure adds approximately 150 million barrels to the supply deficit, with strategic petroleum reserves across OECD nations providing only a temporary buffer.

body.heading: Investment Implications
body.content: For portfolio managers, the Hormuz closure demands a rapid reassessment of energy exposure. Non-Hormuz producers are the clearest beneficiaries: Norwegian energy equities, Brazilian pre-salt operators, and Central Asian pipeline plays offer direct exposure to the supply premium. Energy importers face a more complex calculus. Korean equities — already devastated by the KOSPI crash — may represent deep value if the strait reopens, but the timing risk is extreme. Defensive positioning through gold, US Treasuries, and USD-denominated assets reflects the consensus trade. The longer the closure persists, the more the scenario shifts from a temporary disruption to a structural repricing of Middle Eastern geopolitical risk — with implications for every energy-importing economy's growth trajectory and central bank policy path.
